content.text: What a Fault Domain Is

A fault domain is a physical grouping of servers that share the same power source and top-of-rack network switch. If the power distribution unit (PDU) for a rack row fails, or if the top-of-rack (ToR) switch loses power or develops a hardware fault, every server connected to those components goes offline simultaneously. This simultaneous failure is the blast radius of a single fault domain.

Azure uses fault domains to communicate the physical risk boundary to you. When you place VMs in an Availability Set, Azure tracks fault domain assignments and guarantees that not all VMs in the set land in the same fault domain. This ensures that a single PDU or switch failure cannot take down all copies of your application.

attrs.alt: Fault Domain failure: PDU and ToR switch in FD0 fail, taking all FD0 VMs offline. FD1 and FD2 VMs continue serving.

content.text: Physical Composition of a Fault Domain

A fault domain typically maps to one physical rack, but it can include multiple racks that share a PDU chain or switch pair. The exact physical boundaries are not published by Microsoft (for security reasons), but the guarantee is consistent: resources in different fault domains will not fail together due to a shared power or network component.
Power Distribution Unit (PDU):

a single PDU serves one side of a rack row. If the PDU fails, every server plugged into it loses power. Dual-corded servers with separate PDU feeds can survive one PDU failure, but single-corded servers (common in hyperscale) cannot.
Top-of-Rack (ToR) switch:
each rack has a ToR switch aggregating all server NICs at 100 Gbps. A ToR failure disconnects all servers on that rack from the network, even if they still have power.
CRAC unit (cooling):

a computer room air conditioning unit failure can cause rapid thermal shutdown across multiple racks. Azure's hot/cold aisle containment limits the blast radius, but it is another shared component within a fault domain.

Fault domains protect against hardware and power failures. They do not protect against software failures (bugs in a shared OS image or agent), configuration failures (a bad deployment that hits all VMs), or zone-level infrastructure failures (fire, flood, or power outage for an entire datacenter building). For those, use update domains and Availability Zones respectively.
Fault Domains in Availability Sets

When you create an Availability Set, you choose a platform-fault-domain-count between 1 and 3. With 3 fault domains and 6 VMs, Azure places 2 VMs per fault domain. A fault domain failure takes down the 2 VMs in that domain but leaves the remaining 4 running across the other 2 domains.

# Create an Availability Set with 3 fault domains
az vm availability-set create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAS \
  --location eastus \
  --platform-fault-domain-count 3 \
  --platform-update-domain-count 5

# Deploy 6 VMs — Azure distributes across FDs automatically
for i in $(seq 1 6); do
  az vm create \
    --resource-group myRG \
    --name myVM-$i \
    --availability-set myAS \
    --image Ubuntu2204 \
    --size Standard_DS2_v2 \
    --no-wait \
    --admin-username azureuser \
    --generate-ssh-keys
done

# Wait for all VMs to finish provisioning
az vm wait --resource-group myRG --name myVM-1 --created
az vm wait --resource-group myRG --name myVM-6 --created

# Verify fault domain assignments
for i in $(seq 1 6); do
  FD=$(az vm show -g myRG -n myVM-$i --query "instanceView.platformFaultDomain" -o tsv 2>/dev/null)
  echo "myVM-$i: Fault Domain $FD"
done
Fault Domain Limits and Configuration
Region Type
Max Fault Domains
Notes
Standard Azure region (no AZs)
2 or 3 (region-dependent)
Check az vm availability-set list-sizes for the region
Azure region with Availability Zones
3 per AS
Fault domains within a single AZ; AZs provide broader isolation
Azure Government regions
2 or 3
Same model; check per-region limits

# Check maximum fault domains supported in a region for a given VM size
az vm availability-set list-sizes \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAS \
  --query "[0].{maxFaultDomainCount:numberOfFaultDomains}" \
  --output json

# Alternatively, check the max for a region directly
az compute availability-set list-types \
  --location eastus \
  --query "[*].{type:name, maxFD:maxPlatformFaultDomainCount}" \
  --output table 2>/dev/null || echo "Command varies by CLI version; use portal or docs"

# Show current AS configuration
az vm availability-set show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAS \
  --query "{name:name, faultDomains:platformFaultDomainCount, updateDomains:platformUpdateDomainCount}"
Storage Fault Domains and Managed Disks

Managed disks have their own fault domain concept independent of VM fault domains. When you use an Availability Set with managed disks (sku: Aligned), Azure automatically aligns each disk's fault domain with the fault domain of the VM it is attached to. This prevents the scenario where a VM is in FD0 and its disk is in FD1 — a storage fault domain failure would then take the disk offline while the VM is still running, causing an I/O failure rather than a clean failover.

With unmanaged disks (legacy storage accounts), this alignment does not happen. The storage cluster hosting the VHD file is independent of the VM's compute fault domain. This is why unmanaged disks are strongly discouraged for production workloads.

# Check that all VMs use managed disks (managed disk: non-null managedDisk property)
az vm list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[?availabilitySet!=null].{name:name, osDiskType:storageProfile.osDisk.managedDisk.storageAccountType}" \
  --output table

# Create a VM with an explicit Premium SSD managed disk in an AS
az vm create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM-premium \
  --availability-set myAS \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --size Standard_DS2_v2 \
  --os-disk-size-gb 128 \
  --storage-sku Premium_LRS \
  --admin-username azureuser \
  --generate-ssh-keys

# Attach an additional managed data disk to a VM in an AS
az vm disk attach \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --vm-name myVM-premium \
  --name myDataDisk \
  --size-gb 512 \
  --sku Premium_LRS \
  --new
Fault Domains in Virtual Machine Scale Sets

VMSS (Virtual Machine Scale Sets) support fault domain spreading directly, without requiring an Availability Set. When you set --zones or configure platform-fault-domain-count on a VMSS, Azure spreads instances across fault domains automatically as it scales out. This is the preferred approach for stateless, auto-scaling workloads.

# Create a VMSS spread across fault domains in a single zone
az vmss create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVMSS \
  --image Ubuntu2204 \
  --vm-sku Standard_DS2_v2 \
  --instance-count 6 \
  --platform-fault-domain-count 3 \
  --single-placement-group false

# Scale up (new instances will be distributed across FDs)
az vmss scale \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVMSS \
  --new-capacity 9

# List instances with their fault domain assignments
az vmss list-instances \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVMSS \
  --query "[*].{instance:instanceId, fd:instanceView.platformFaultDomain}" \
  --output table
Setting single-placement-group: false on a VMSS is required for more than 100 instances and unlocks the 3-fault-domain limit. A single placement group VMSS is limited to 2 fault domains.
Fault Domains vs Availability Zones: Scope Comparison
Fault domains and Availability Zones both exist to prevent correlated failures, but they operate at different physical scales:
Attribute
Fault Domain
Availability Zone
Physical scope
One rack or rack cluster (shared PDU + ToR switch)
One or more entire datacenter buildings
Failure type protected
PDU failure, ToR switch failure, single-rack cooling failure
Building power, cooling, or network failure; physical disasters
How many
2-3 per Availability Set or region
3 per region (where AZs exist)
Distance between domains
Same building; meters apart
Different buildings; kilometers apart
VM-to-VM latency within
Sub-millisecond
1-2ms
Azure mechanism
Availability Set (compute) + managed disk alignment (storage)
Zone flag on VMs/VMSS or zone-redundant service SKU
SLA contribution
99.95% (with 2+ VMs in AS)
99.99% (with 2+ VMs across zones)
Programmatically Verifying Fault Domain Distribution

After deploying VMs in an Availability Set, verify that Azure actually placed them in different fault domains. Azure usually does this automatically, but edge cases (capacity constraints, region limits) can cause suboptimal placement.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Audit fault domain distribution for all VMs in an Availability Set
RG="${1:-myRG}"
AS="${2:-myAS}"

echo "Checking VMs in Availability Set: $AS"
echo "---"

# Get all VMs in the resource group that belong to the AS
AS_ID=$(az vm availability-set show -g "$RG" -n "$AS" --query id -o tsv)

az vm list -g "$RG" \
  --query "[?availabilitySet.id=='$AS_ID'].name" \
  -o tsv | while read VM; do
  FD=$(az vm get-instance-view -g "$RG" -n "$VM" \
    --query "instanceView.platformFaultDomain" -o tsv 2>/dev/null)
  UD=$(az vm get-instance-view -g "$RG" -n "$VM" \
    --query "instanceView.platformUpdateDomain" -o tsv 2>/dev/null)
  STATUS=$(az vm get-instance-view -g "$RG" -n "$VM" \
    --query "instanceView.statuses[1].displayStatus" -o tsv 2>/dev/null)
  echo "$VM  FD=$FD  UD=$UD  Status=$STATUS"
done

echo ""
echo "Expected: no two VMs with the same FD (if VMs <= FD count)"
echo "AS config: $(az vm availability-set show -g $RG -n $AS --query '{faultDomains:platformFaultDomainCount,updateDomains:platformUpdateDomainCount}' -o json)"
Common Misconceptions
Fault domains are not the same as Availability Zones:
an AZ protects against whole-building failures. A fault domain protects against rack-level failures within a building. AZs contain fault domains.
More VMs than fault domains is still valuable:
with 6 VMs across 3 fault domains, 2 VMs share each FD. A fault domain failure takes 2 VMs offline — not all 6. Two-thirds of capacity remains.
You cannot choose a specific fault domain:
Azure assigns fault domains automatically when VMs are added to an Availability Set. You specify the count; the platform assigns specific VMs.
Fault domains only work with Availability Sets (for VMs):
standalone VMs (not in an AS) have no fault domain guarantee. Azure may or may not place them in separate racks from each other — there is no guarantee.
Fault domains are fixed at AS creation:
you cannot change the platform-fault-domain-count after the Availability Set is created. If you need a different count, you must create a new AS and migrate VMs to it.
VMSS uses fault domains differently:
VMSS can use fault domains without an Availability Set, controlled by the --platform-fault-domain-count on the VMSS itself.
Fault Domain Failure Scenarios and Recovery

Understanding what happens during a fault domain failure helps you design the right response at the load balancer and application layers. The timeline from failure to recovered state involves multiple components acting in sequence.
Unplanned Fault Domain Failure Timeline
Physical failure occurs: PDU loses power or ToR switch hardware fault. All servers in the rack lose connectivity within milliseconds.
VM state changes: Azure's host agent detects that VMs on the affected hosts are unreachable. VM status changes to 'Stopped (Unavailable)' within 1-5 minutes depending on detection timing.

Load balancer health probe failure: Standard Load Balancer health probes fire every 5 seconds (default). After 2 consecutive failures (10 seconds), the VM is removed from the backend pool. Traffic stops being sent to the failed VMs.
Traffic redistribution: the remaining VMs absorb the load. If they were already handling significant traffic, this may trigger autoscaling events.
Azure remediation: Microsoft's datacenter operations team is alerted. Hardware is inspected, replaced, and servers are rebooted. This can take minutes to hours depending on the failure type.
VM restart: once the host comes back online, Azure restarts the VMs that were running. The VMs come back to their last state (OS and data disks intact; in-memory state lost).
Return to backend pool: health probes start succeeding; the LB re-adds the VMs to the backend pool.

# Configure LB health probe with shorter detection window (2 probes * 5s = 10s to detect failure)
az network lb probe create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --lb-name myLB \
  --name myHealthProbe \
  --protocol Http \
  --port 80 \
  --path /health \
  --interval 5 \
  --threshold 2

# Set up an alert for VM unavailability events in Resource Health
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name "VMUnavailableAlert" \
  --scopes "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)/resourceGroups/myRG" \
  --condition "category=ResourceHealth and status=Inactive" \
  --action-groups /subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/myRG/providers/microsoft.insights/actionGroups/myAG

# Check current status of VMs after suspected fault domain failure
az vm list -g myRG --show-details \
  --query "[*].{name:name, powerState:powerState, provState:provisioningState}" \
  --output table
Application-Level Resilience During Fault Domain Failures

The load balancer handles traffic routing automatically, but your application must also handle connections that were in-flight when the fault domain failed. TCP connections to VMs in the failed domain are abruptly terminated — the client will see a connection reset or timeout. Well-designed applications handle this with:
Retry logic with exponential backoff:
retry failed requests with increasing delays (e.g., 100ms, 200ms, 400ms) to absorb the brief window while the LB detects and removes the failed VM.
Circuit breakers:
detect that a downstream endpoint is failing and stop sending requests to it without waiting for the full timeout, reducing latency during the failure window.
Idempotent operations:
if a request failed mid-execution on the crashed VM, retrying it on a healthy VM must not cause duplicate side effects (double charges, duplicate records). Design operations to be idempotent.
Session affinity awareness:
if the LB uses session affinity (sticky sessions), a fault domain failure severs all sessions pinned to the failed VM. Either use stateless VMs or have clients re-establish sessions gracefully.
Fault Domains in the Context of the SLA

The 99.95% Availability Set SLA is underwritten partly by fault domain isolation and partly by update domain protection. Fault domain isolation ensures unplanned hardware failures in a single rack do not cause complete downtime. The SLA guarantees that at least one VM in a 2-VM AS is reachable for 99.95% of each month.

The math works like this: if each physical rack has an unplanned outage rate of, say, 0.1% per month (52 minutes), and your VMs are in separate fault domains, both racks would need to fail simultaneously for your application to be down. If rack failures are independent, the probability of both failing in the same minute is 0.1% * 0.1% = 0.0001% — far below the SLA floor.

Azure's SLA is a Service Level Agreement, not a Service Level Guarantee. If Microsoft fails to meet it, you receive a service credit — you do not get your downtime back. Design for the SLA you need, not the SLA Azure offers, by layering redundancy beyond what the SLA minimally requires.
Fault Domains for Azure Managed Disks (Without VMs)

Managed disks also have fault domains, independent of any VM. When you create a managed disk without specifying a zone, Azure places it in one of its storage stamp fault domains. If you then create a VM in an Availability Set and attach the disk, Azure attempts to align the disk's storage fault domain with the VM's compute fault domain.
You can also query the fault domain of a standalone managed disk:

# Show the fault domain of a managed disk
az disk show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myDataDisk \
  --query "{name:name, faultDomain:diskProperties.diskState, sku:sku.name, zone:zones}" \
  --output json

# Create a zone-pinned managed disk (fault domain is within the zone)
az disk create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myZonalDisk \
  --size-gb 256 \
  --sku Premium_LRS \
  --zone 1   # Zone 1 has its own fault domains; this disk lives in one of them

# List all managed disks and their zones/fault domain info
az disk list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --query "[*].{name:name, sku:sku.name, zone:zones[0], sizeGB:diskSizeGb}" \
  --output table
Fault Domains and Kubernetes on Azure

When running AKS (Azure Kubernetes Service), the underlying node VMs can be spread across fault domains using node pool zone configuration. The AKS control plane is always zone-redundant in supported regions, but the node pools must be explicitly configured for zone spreading.

Kubernetes itself has a pod topology spread constraint that can distribute pods across nodes in different fault domains (or zones), providing application-level fault domain awareness on top of the infrastructure-level fault domain spreading.

# Create an AKS cluster with node pool spread across zones (and therefore fault domains)
az aks create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myAKS \
  --node-count 3 \
  --zones 1 2 3 \
  --enable-managed-identity

# Add a user node pool with zone spreading
az aks nodepool add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --cluster-name myAKS \
  --name workloads \
  --node-count 6 \
  --zones 1 2 3

# Kubernetes pod topology spread constraint for fault domain awareness
# Apply this to your Deployment or StatefulSet
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapp
spec:
  replicas: 6
  template:
    spec:
      topologySpreadConstraints:
        - maxSkew: 1
          topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone   # Spread across AZs
          whenUnsatisfiable: DoNotSchedule
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: myapp
        - maxSkew: 1
          topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname        # Also spread across nodes (fault domains)
          whenUnsatisfiable: ScheduleAnyway
          labelSelector:
            matchLabels:
              app: myapp
      containers:
        - name: myapp
          image: myapp:latest

topologyKey: topology.kubernetes.io/zone spreads pods across Availability Zones. topologyKey: kubernetes.io/hostname spreads pods across individual nodes, which in turn are spread across fault domains when the node pool uses --zones. Combining both gives zone-level and fault-domain-level pod distribution.
Summary

A fault domain is the blast radius of a shared physical component — typically a rack's power distribution unit and top-of-rack switch. When one fails, all servers sharing those components fail together. Azure's Availability Set mechanism ensures that VMs are spread across separate fault domains, so no single rack failure can take down all copies of a workload.
Fault domains map to physical rack boundaries: shared PDU and ToR switch define the failure scope.
Availability Sets with 3 fault domains guarantee VMs land in separate racks, limiting a single PDU/switch failure to a subset of VMs.
Use managed disks with sku: Aligned so disk fault domains are co-located with VM fault domains — preventing an isolated disk failure from appearing as a VM failure.
VMSS supports fault domain spreading natively without requiring a separate Availability Set.
Fault domains protect against rack-level hardware failures only; Availability Zones provide the next level of isolation for datacenter-building failures.
After deploying VMs into an AS, verify fault domain assignments with az vm get-instance-view to confirm expected spreading.
